media.caption: The Hubble Space Telescope photographed during its final servicing mission.

text.headline: A Brief History of Light
text.text: From the first photons after the Big Bang to humanity's greatest telescopes. This timeline mixes cosmological-scale dates with ordinary human ones — a test case for the date-formatting fix.

media.caption: WMAP all-sky map of the cosmic microwave background — the afterglow of the Big Bang.

text.headline: The Big Bang
text.text: Around 380,000 years after the Big Bang, the universe cooled enough for the first photons to travel freely — the cosmos became transparent to light for the first time.

media.caption: An artist's impression of a protoplanetary disk.

text.headline: The Sun Ignites
text.text: Our Sun — a middling G-type star — begins nuclear fusion, flooding the inner solar system with light.

media.caption: Trilobites were among the first creatures with complex eyes.

text.headline: The Cambrian Explosion and the First Complex Eyes
text.text: Complex, image-forming eyes appear in the fossil record almost simultaneously across dozens of animal lineages. Light becomes a sense.

media.caption: A reconstruction of Homo erectus, close ancestor of modern humans.

text.headline: Homo sapiens Appear
text.text: Anatomically modern humans emerge in Africa. With large brains and forward-facing eyes, we become the first species to contemplate the stars.

text.headline: First Manufactured Glass
text.text: Egyptians and Mesopotamians produce the first manufactured glass and glazed faience — humanity begins to shape transparent materials and manipulate light deliberately.

text.headline: Euclid's Optica
text.text: Euclid writes the first systematic treatment of optics, describing how vision works through straight lines (rays) traveling from the eye to objects — wrong direction, but the geometry was right.

text.headline: Ibn al-Haytham's Book of Optics
text.text: The polymath correctly explains that light travels from objects to the eye, describes the camera obscura, and lays the foundation for modern optics and the scientific method.

media.caption: Galileo's observations of Jupiter's moons from Sidereus Nuncius, 1610.

text.headline: Galileo Points a Telescope at the Moon
text.text: In November 1609, Galileo turns his improved refracting telescope skyward. He sees mountains on the Moon, moons around Jupiter, and stars invisible to the naked eye — the universe suddenly grows.

media.caption: View from the Window at Le Gras — the oldest surviving photograph.

text.headline: The First Photograph
text.text: Nicéphore Niépce captures the oldest surviving photograph using an 8-hour exposure. Light can now be permanently recorded.

media.caption: Buzz Aldrin on the Moon, July 20, 1969.

text.headline: Apollo 11: Humans Walk on the Moon
text.text: Neil Armstrong and Buzz Aldrin land on the Sea of Tranquility. The mission's Hasselblad cameras produce some of history's most iconic photographs, taken in the light of a sun 150 million kilometers away.

media.caption: The Hubble Space Telescope — above Earth's blurring atmosphere, it collects ancient light from galaxies billions of light-years away.

text.headline: Hubble Space Telescope Deployed
text.text: STS-31 deploys the Hubble Space Telescope on April 25, 1990. Above Earth's blurring atmosphere, it collects ancient light from galaxies billions of light-years away, closing the circle that began with the Big Bang.
